sentences.text: Hello.
My name is Abraham Jachzel.
It's J-A-C-H-Z-E-L.
Yes, so it's all right.
The date of my birth?
Trzebinia.
T-R-Z-E-B-I-N-I-A, something like this.
In Poland.
Krakow.
Near Krakow, yes.
November 17, 1923.
Golda Klainberger.
Right.
Solomon.
Sloima.
Golda.
I have two sisters.
I had two sisters.
I have one.
The other sister was taken with my mother.
Taken with my mother.
Well my older sister, Eidela, was born September 14, 1927.
Eidela, yes.
Eidela.
Huja.
Hadassah.
And--
Her birthday was 1931.
Some-- sometime around the holidays.
Something like that.
No, No.
My mother came from that city.
They got married, and they moved out.
Krakow.
Yes.
I did.
It was very nice for a young boy.
It was very nice.
It was-- you go-- you go to school, then you get a little-- a little cheder.
And you know, a city with lights, with movies, with theatre, and stuff like this.
It was-- it was-- it was a beautiful city.
For me, it was a good life.
Yes, the Jewish community was the old section of Krakow.
They call it Casimir.
The name is after the King Casimir, who brought the Jews, I think, from Armenia.
In, I think it was 1450, something like this.
And there were-- they just like to live close to each other, you know?
And this was about the Jewish Quarter.
I could say, there were, like, 90% Jews and 10% Polacks.
In that area, was about a square mile, whatever.
My-- yes, I lived with my family.
In the Jewish Quarters.
The beginning, right on the corner where Krakowska starts, you know.
It was very nice.
The Jews were in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] their own-- their own buildings.
The-- the trade, the-- the sheet metal trade, the garment trade, the-- I mean, anything.
The Jews were the educators.
Most-- you know, the doctors, the lawyers.
In Krakow.
And there were teachers not only for us.
I mean, there were teachers for the Polish schools.
I went to public school.
It was, first I went with-- it was Jews and Polacks.
And then, I went-- there was only Jewish school, only boys.
And it-- it was closer.
And I don't know, my father liked it.
I didn't care for it, but he want it this way.
About the seventh grade.
Yes.
I must have started very early, you know?
Before, before the school, they already send me to the cheder all the time.
From three-- oh, I-- I used to-- after bar mitzvah, I used to cheat already, you know?
I-- let's say I didn't like too much the cheder.
I wasn't too anxious about it.
They had very great teachers.
And I-- I like-- I was occupied.
I don't know, I had-- I wanted to go play soccer, instead.
I wanted I wanted to be-- a little thing, you know?
I don't know.
I-- For some kind of reason, I was [LAUGHS] the black sheep in a religious family, who just didn't like the cheder.
It-- it-- and it didn't go into my head, either.
You know, I wasn't a good student.
Right.
Leshon Hakodesh.
It was Leshon Hakodesh.
OK, so it was not a Zionist sort of Hebrew school?
No, no, oh, no.
There was a Rabbi.
Not a Rabbi, it was a, how do you call it?
A melamed, yeah.
And he used to punish us.
And he used to screw it in, and it didn't go in my head too good.
It didn't work out.
Yes, they decided since they cannot do anything with me, you know, they're going to send me to a Hebrew school, where I had to learn a little Hebrew, and mechanical high school, right.
It was an expensive school.
They had to pay monthly, you know, whatever it was.
And we had, let's see, four hours Hebrew a week, and we had about 20 hours working in the shop work.
Then we had another, 20 hours, you know, school.
There were lathes.
You had to go through everything.
You-- you were, like, two weeks, you know, you work with the welders.
You-- about four weeks each year, each semester, you had to work with lathes the and the milling machines, and then all kind of hand work, you know, filing and stuff like this.
And then we had one month of blacksmith work.
It was-- we didn't exactly horseshoe horses, but it was a part.
It was nice.
Mechanical.
And we had a lot metallurgy to learn and everything.
It was very helpful to get started.
I had to work between schools, between semesters.
You had to show that you did something.
They would work for some kind of-- some kind of mechanical shop or something, you know, to get a little more practice outside.
Apprentice, you know, outside.
So you're not strange when you go and look for a job.
So--
I worked for a-- it was a-- it was a good place.
Mr.
Gemski, ?] and we did-- I remember we did-- we did the front gate for the university-- Jagiellonian University of Krakow.
Jagiellonian, that's the oldest university in Krakow.
We did the gate.
And-- and this was an art.
Since he put the metal flowers on it.
And then you put the green color on it, it's without paint, you know?
Some kind of chemicals he used and fire to get-- to get the colors.
This was the secrets, you know.
Every-- every trade had a secret, and he was one of the-- one of the best.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Every trade was Jewish.
They-- they were-- the rest was mostly farmers.
I mean, the trades were Jewish.
All the-- all the maker-- all that was mechanics, all that was making leather, you know, leather goods or garments or-- you know, the whole-- the-- the whole industry.
I-- there was very few factories, you know, Polish factories around Krakow.
Even those big ones, they were partly Jewish, you know, like the nail factories and--
No, no.
The one in Krakow?
They were-- they were-- they were merchants.
They were doing-- no, they were everything.
But, you know, outside, you know?
Krakow was combined.
It was-- happens to be-- this was already the big city with 240,000 people, which I understand now it's got a million people.
And-- and all mostly surrounded with farmland.
And you know, the farmland-- now, the farmers used to come in to sell the products and to buy-- to spend their money at the stores, mostly.
Especially, they used to come to the Jewish quarters.
They could-- they got a better deal.
We had a-- we had a shop.
We did some stamping.
And we did-- it-- it was a-- the company name was Metalosport.
We did sport items, and we did some-- most of it, it was jewelry for horses for-- for the bridals.
And it was a good-- it was an item, I never saw it in the United States.
The horses I see at the race track, they don't have anything on it.
You know, the weight means a lot.
They're-- they're-- they're-- they're just as beautiful, and maybe more beautiful.
And I'm sure they're more expensive, and they don't have any jewelry.
No, every Polack wanted to arrive or to dress up.
You know, he was going to church and he was-- and then, the taxis.
The-- the-- how would you call the--
No, no, it wouldn't be balagula.
But if you want to go to the train, there were no taxis in-- in 1930s, you know?
There were stand up, one-- I tell you, across the street where we were living, they were standing in line.
And if you wanted to go to, you know, to the railroad, or you want to go bring something to somebody, or you visit some guests, you took those buggies.
I mean, you didn't take them, you hire.
And in line, you had to take the first one, you know?
They had like a union between themselves.
And those horses, everybody tried to dress up and polish his buggy as nice as he could, and see that every couple years he puts a coat of paint on it, you know.
So it was an item.
It was an item that families-- we made a beautiful living out of it.
And then, in about 1935, '36, my father started-- came out with an idea we're going to do some sport items.
So we started with ski poles, you know, bamboo, and the other kind of stuff.
And we used to put the-- the handles on it, and the wheels, and the points, and the-- you know, and we sold it.
And we saw, hey, listen, when we can't do this, we can do the other thing.
Since we have presses, let's do the bindings.
So we started doing bindings.
And then we started doing a little more of everything.
And then there were some people who couldn't pay after the season.
And if my father wind up with gloves, and-- and sweaters, and other stuff in exchanges.
Hey, look, let's go all the way, you know.
And we did very good.
Till we did-- we came-- we ordered the skis And we put our name on it, you know?
We named it Texas Hickory.
Texas?
But I don't even know where Texas is and what hickory means.
But somebody put it together a name.
And they were good.
And people would start asking for that name.
It was--
We did a good living.
No, I don't think so.
I want to go there.
I want to know what's-- what happened, you know?
I know when-- when we had to go to the ghetto, and we had to start moving around, you know, get rid of it.
My father split.
He give-- like the dies he split them.
They give-- there were always enemies between-- between the workers.
So he gave them the tops and the other ones the bottom, so they couldn't get together and start a business by themself.
And after the war, they came back, and they start working.
You know, start doing a little bit.
But my father didn't have it in him anymore, you know?
He lost that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know-- The drive.
--how they say in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You know?
He said, ah, let's do something else.
And in Krakow, he did start organizing for the city.
You know, the Jewish things, you know?
This is after the war?
After the war.
Are we going too fast?
OK, let's go.
We're going-- we don't want to go after the war yet.
OK, we didn't go there.
Well, I know, he was a Belz Hasidut That's all he belonged, you know, my father.
Whoever, you know--
I want to tell you my uncle was the rosh yeshiva in Auschwitz.
Oswiecim, but this is Auschwitz.
You know, but there was a city, and there was a part what was the camp-- the concentration camp.
There's another part.
The name is the same.
Oh, yeah, oh, yeah.
He schlepped me with me, too, you know.
By the ear.
I hated it.
I-- I-- I know-- I know the-- the Belzer Rebbe, you know, used to-- used-- I remember he-- he had trouble with his eyes.
He was across the street. on [INAUDIBLE].

Where we used to live just across the street by [INAUDIBLE] by-- while he was attending to the doctor, you know, and all those thousands of people standing in front of the house tried to get-- to get in, you know, just to see him.
But I was-- my father-- he was roaming with all of them.
I don't know.
I-- I know-- I know Rabbi [INAUDIBLE], you know.
I know-- I know the one in Brooklyn.
I know his father.
He had the quarters in Trzebinia, where I used to go to my grandmother on vacation every summer.
Where I was born.
So my grandmother was still there, so we used to go there to my grandmother.
He was.
He could have been-- he had the credentials to be a rabbi, let's say that.
He's-- he was a-- a big learner.
He made a living out of it in United States.
No.
What happened-- he came to the United States, so he became a [INAUDIBLE].
It was down in the Bronx.
One time I came home from California.
I said, hey, what's going on here?
Big write-up, you know?
My name on it, you know?
European [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] [LAUGHTER] But she said, oh, that-- that-- that-- that's how they want it.
That's how they wrote it up.
So anyway, my father moved from one section to-- or are we still supposed to talk about-- It's OK.
It's alright, alright.
This was an interesting sideline.
I was interested.
Anyway, he was working for ARCO and had an accident.
And he was-- then he collected-- how you call it?
When you have an accident, how you call this?
Workman's compensation.
And then on Grand Concourse, he-- he was-- he became a-- a regular [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] .
And across the street, there-- there-- there was a court, you know.
That was 161st Street.
There's a court-- a big court of the Bronx is on Grand Concourse.
The judges, the lawyers, they used to come into the shul.
And he was learning [INAUDIBLE] with them.
So anyway, I will never understand.
My father always had a pocket full with money.
Somehow, he did better than anybody, you know?
My father.
I was the black sheep in the whole family.
I-- I just-- I just didn't-- I have nothing against it.
I love it very much.
I have a very-- I have a respect for all the rabbis for everything.
It just didn't go with me.
You know, it just--
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah, but you know-- but--
Krakow had-- you have to understand that Krakow had about 60,000 Jews.
On top of it, there was at least 20,000 Hasidim, which were not registered.
And that-- they couldn't-- you could-- you can sleep in my house but you have to register.
It mostly was apartment houses.
When somebody came over to sleep, even your relative or somebody, you had to register him.
And next day, it was in the-- in the books, you know?
Not police station, in the book.
And they had to register in the station.
Whatever.
Each house had a book, you know, apartment house had a book.
Somebody came in.
I say, OK, he's going to stay over one night.
So all right, so he-- But if he stood up-- let's say, my uncle came from Trzebinia.
He had to stay for a week.
He had to go to court or someplace else.
So he registered and everything.
And then when you left out, you know, you had to register out from that he was that.
But the Hasidim, they didn't have to register, since they were sleeping on the benches.
In the-- in the shul.
There were 500 shuls, you know, in Krakow.
Some of them 400, 500 years old.
Oh, the Alta Shul.
The Old Shul, you know?
And well, there was--
The Old Shul?
Must be like, 450, 400.
I know, anyway, it was built on a flat land.
And it was sinking all the time.
So now, when we-- even when we were kids, they explained to us why there was like a all around, like a little park.
And then you had to go down steps, like eight, 10 steps.
It's a museum now, I hear, the Old Shul.
The Remuh?
The Remuh Shul is a little [NON-ENGLISH] It's small.
I don't think that more than 200-- 100 or 200 people could-- I don't even think so-- could get in.
No, no, no.
No, this was in a different quarters, you know, different part of-- it's still in the ghetto city, you know, whatever it is, but it was different kind.
He, Dad spoke to us Yiddish, and we were answering in Polish.
Yes, yeah, yeah, yeah, my sisters, too.
Yeah.
It was-- it was regular.
It was the same as any other place, I think.
But at least it didn't show.
There was anti-Semitism, especially when we lived in the quarters on Casimir, yeah.
And the last two years, they used to come up and-- don't buy by the Jews, and they used to break, you know, the windows.
But the Jews were organized, and then you have to hit it back.
And there was time that--
I'm talking '38, beginning of '39, as the pressure start coming in.
Yes, I remember, you know, they-- they broke a few windows, you know, in the stores, but they hardly came out alive, you know?
Came out alive.
But you know, they start, first time, they'll let them in.
They broke it.
The second time they came in, they were waiting for them.
Had to protect themselves, and they had their ways.
No, When I was-- from beginning, I was in a regular school, regular public school.
No.
The only thing is, you know, there was a rabbi came in once a week for an hour, for us.
It wasn't a rabbi.
It was a Hebrew teacher they would call.
And then, you know, we were lucky, but you know, when the priest come in and they were teaching them, so we were outside, you know.
So we had-- we had free--
Yes.
Not a rabbi.
Not a melamed.
It was like a teacher.
Hebrew teacher, they would call it, you know, came in.
So we had an hour in-- in the first grade, an hour in the second grade, you know?
Let's say there was two classes, or first two classes or second and third, and so far.
But the priest had a little more, you know?
They had a little more than one-- one hour, you know?
They had-- maybe they had a bigger budget.
So since we weren't such good kids, they made us stay with the priest.
They say we were running around too much.
And-- and it-- it was funny, you know?
Some of them didn't-- didn't remember something, you know, from the Catholic [INAUDIBLE], and he'd say, look, they used to ask us, and we knew it.
So he'd say, you see?
That Jewish kid knows.
You don't know it, you know.
Can you just tell me, was your home-- obviously it was  a kosher home.
Yes.
Oh, Shabbos was special in Krakow, you know?
I don't care-- even the people who never went to shul, who didn't-- who-- who were whatever.
They would never had the guts to get up on a streetcar, you know.
And if somebody-- I remember, you know, if you got out on a streetcar.
Nobody was around.
You looked around.
Nobody jumped.
Then you put your head down.
So God forbid somebody sees you, you know, because they're open, you know?
No, it was very-- Friday night, I remember-- even a half hour before the [INAUDIBLE] you know, they used to come around and knock.
Shabbos!
Shabbos!
Close the windows!
You know, all that thing, you know?
This, I remember.
And you know, the Jewish merchants, some of them open up after the-- you know, after the-- after Shabbos, they'd open up for a few hours, and then they want to open up on Sunday.
It wasn't-- it was forbidden.
They used to bring them through the back, you know, and try to sell everything.
It was a big fine and so far.
Oh, boy, my-- my-- my-- my father, you know, there wasn't a Pesach, there shouldn't be a fight.
He said, how much?
You know, when he started [INAUDIBLE] it was an hour and a half, and it was only 12 words, you know?
Oh, yeah, sure, OK.
Yes.
Oh, yeah.
They were younger.
They were behaving themselves a little better than me.
And you have to have the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what I mean?
When you picked up something you didn't want to eat before, you know, you had the salt shaker in your hand.
It was very strict.
Oh, the bracha, and the Kiddush and the-- and the-- Havdalah, you know, this-- this-- this had to be in every house. as far as the houses I used to come in.
No.
[LAUGHS] Matter of fact, I saw a Jewish theater on Second Avenue about 20 years ago, maybe more.
And they had the Havdalah, and already I had tears in my eyes.
I didn't hear so many years, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a signal.
Yeah.
